formations_blocks: Egypt starting with Salah as a No.10 behind Marmoush, with Ziko on the right and Ashour on the left
key_matchups: Kevin De Bruyne shooting from the edge of the D against Egypt's goalkeeper Shobeir
Salah challenging Onana, who goes down after contact
phases.summary: Egypt begin brightly and on the front foot, with Salah operating as a No.10 behind Marmoush and supporting wide attackers on either side. Belgium’s main early threat comes from De Bruyne, who already finds a pocket at the top of the box for a curling effort, while the only notable physical duel mentioned is Salah catching Onana.

formations_blocks: Egypt in a compact defensive setup with counterattacking moments
adjustments: Ashour moved infield from the left to find space before the goal
Salah playing angled passes into central areas on transition
Belgium increased attacking pressure after the goal but remained wasteful
phases.summary: This phase was tight early on, with Belgium slightly on top after a slow start but Egypt threatening in transition. The decisive tactical moment came when Ashour drifted in from the left to receive Salah’s angled pass and finish from the edge of the D, while Belgium’s response produced more pressure than clear chances. Doku and De Bruyne offered moments of threat, but Egypt’s compactness and direct counterattacking gave them the better end product.

adjustments: Salah delayed the counter and recycled possession to a defensive teammate
Egypt chose to slow the tempo and see the match through to half-time
phases.summary: Egypt ended the half by prioritising control over risk, with Salah’s break being recycled rather than forced forward once support was lacking. The phase points to a cautious defensive approach as Egypt protected their 1-0 lead at the break against Belgium.

formations_blocks: Egypt defending in a compact block and doubling up on Doku
Belgium looking to isolate Doku in wide duels and attack through set pieces
adjustments: Belgium made a double substitution on 57' with Maxim De Cuyper and Nicolas Raskin replacing Timothy Castagne and Amadou Onana
De Bruyne varied Belgium's free-kick routine by going short once before later taking one direct
Egypt responded to Belgium's pressure by tightening coverage on Doku with repeated doubling
phases.summary: This phase was defined by Belgium trying to unlock Egypt through Doku’s 1v1s and De Bruyne’s set pieces, while Egypt stayed disciplined enough to double up on Belgium’s main wide threat. Belgium’s adjustments came via a double change in midfield/defence, but Egypt also created dangerous transitions and chances through Marmoush and Salah before Belgium eventually levelled via an own goal.

The available text for this phase is dominated by team-news context rather than in-play tactical detail. Based on the lineups provided, both sides are set up in mirrored 4-2-3-1 shapes, with Belgium and Egypt matching each other structurally rather than showing any documented late-game tactical shifts. No specific press, block, adjustment, or individual matchup is described in the supplied commentary.
